article_title: Go Team Launches Official pkg.go.dev API, Replacing Years of Web Scraping Workarounds
summary: Clean submission; all quotes and claims verified against source snapshots; allowlist warning for pkg.go.dev is a false positive (subdomain of go.dev, which is allowlisted)

content_preview.title: Go Team Launches Official pkg.go.dev API, Replacing Years of Web Scraping Workarounds
content_preview.summary: The Go team published a formal REST API for pkg.go.dev on May 21, giving tool authors and IDE integrations a stable, versioned interface to module metadata, documentation, symbols, and vulnerability data.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Go team on May 21, 2026 published a formal API for pkg.go.dev, the official home for Go package documentation and module discovery. The announcement, authored by Ethan Lee, Hana Kim, and Jonathan Amsterdam, introduces a stable, machine-readable interface that fills a long-standing gap in the Go tooling ecosystem: until now, developers who needed programmatic access to package metadata had no sanctioned path.

"Developers building tools, IDE integrations, and automated workflows ...

recommendations: Consider adding pkg.go.dev to config/source_allowlist.txt as an explicit entry alongside go.dev to suppress future false-positive allowlist warnings

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, well-written 628-word News article covering the Go team's formal pkg.go.dev API launch. The structure is logical: overview, endpoint listing, design philosophy, reference CLI, stability commitments, and known unknowns. Technical depth is appropriate for a developer audience.
editor_notes.source_verification: All three source snapshots read and verified from disk. source-0.html.gz (go.dev/blog/pkgsite-api): confirmed all direct quotes verbatim — 'Developers building tools, IDE integrations, and automated workflows have historically relied on fragile workarounds like web scraping to access this data,' 'Tools can now access the specific, high-fidelity context needed to reason about the Go ecosystem with greater precision,' 'One design principle for this API is precision over convenience,' and the backward compatibility pledge — all appear word-for-word. Authorship (Ethan Lee, Hana Kim, Jonathan Amsterdam) and publication date (21 May 2026) confirmed. source-1.html.gz (pkg.go.dev/api): confirmed API documentation exists and matches all eight endpoint families listed in the article; the GET-only stateless architecture and disambiguation behavior are accurately described. source-2.html.gz (GitHub issue #79453): confirmed the proposal to drop gccgo was filed by adonovan (Alan Donovan) on May 18, 2026, and is described as a 'proposal: drop gccgo.' The article correctly characterizes this as Alan Donovan proposing to discontinue gccgo support. The allowlist warning for pkg.go.dev is a false positive: pkg.go.dev is a subdomain of go.dev, which is explicitly in the allowlist, and is the official Go package registry — the primary subject of this article.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims trace to cited sources. Eight endpoints listed match the official API spec exactly. The install command for pkgsite-cli matches the blog post. The precision-over-convenience design principle, the v1beta path, the OpenAPI YAML location, and the backward compatibility commitment are all verified verbatim. The gccgo proposal description is accurate (proposal to drop, not already dropped; filed concurrently; characterized as unrelated to the API — all confirmed by the snapshot).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no editorializing, no AI self-reference. The 'What We Don't Know' section is a strong editorial addition that correctly flags open questions without speculation.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate coverage found in existing articles. The topic is new to the archive.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality submission ready for immediate publication. All quotes verbatim, all claims sourced, writing is precise and appropriately technical. The article is a model of accurate coverage of a developer tooling announcement.
